title: Measuring Crime
examTip: The 'dark figure of crime' is one of the most frequently examined concepts in this topic — always explain it clearly: crime that occurs but is never reported to or recorded by the police. Use the CSEW as the counter-evidence.
introTips: Official crime statistics = what the police recorded. CSEW = what people actually experienced. They differ enormously.
The 'dark figure' = all the crime that never gets counted.
Self-report studies show crime is spread more evenly across classes than official statistics suggest.
intro: How do we know how much crime there is? It sounds like a simple question, but the answer is surprisingly difficult. Every statistic about crime has been filtered through a series of human decisions — whether to report, whether to record, how to classify. By the time a number appears in an official report, it may tell us more about the police and the public than about crime itself.

keyPoints.heading: Defining Crime and Deviance
keyPoints.content: Deviance is behaviour that breaks the norms of a society — but is not necessarily illegal. Examples: talking loudly on your phone in a quiet library, not clapping at the end of a concert, a man wearing a dress in a conservative community. Crime is behaviour that breaks the formal laws of a society and carries legal punishment (fine, community service, imprisonment). The distinction is important: some deviant acts are not crimes (burping at a formal dinner) and some crimes are not widely seen as deviant by those who commit them (speeding, downloading music illegally). The social construction of crime is a central insight: what counts as criminal is not fixed — it varies across time and culture. Homosexuality was illegal in England and Wales until 1967. Cannabis is legal for recreational use in the Netherlands, illegal in the UK; alcohol is legal in the UK, banned in Saudi Arabia. Women in the UK could not be legally raped by their husbands until 1991. This variation proves that crime is not an absolute, natural category — it is socially defined by those with the power to make and enforce laws.
writeBullets: Deviance — behaviour that breaks social norms; not necessarily illegal
Crime — behaviour that breaks formal laws and carries legal punishment
Some deviance is not crime (burping at dinner); some crime is not seen as deviant (speeding)
Social construction of crime — what is criminal varies across time and cultures
Homosexuality illegal in England and Wales until 1967 — shows crime is historically relative
Cannabis legal in Netherlands, illegal in UK — shows crime is culturally relative
keyPoints.memTip: Crime is a LABEL — society decides what counts. It's not written in the stars.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The social construction argument is also linked to labelling theory (Becker) — cross-reference with s4-explanations-crime.

keyPoints.heading: Official Statistics: Police-Recorded Crime
keyPoints.content: Official crime statistics count crimes that are reported to and recorded by the police, published annually by the Office for National Statistics (ONS). Problems with official statistics are severe. The 'dark figure' of crime refers to all the crime that happens but never makes it into the statistics. Crime goes unreported for many reasons: the victim is embarrassed or ashamed (sexual assaults, domestic violence), the victim fears reprisals (especially in gang contexts), the victim doesn't think the crime is serious enough to bother reporting, the victim distrusts the police (especially prevalent among ethnic minority communities following events like the Macpherson Report's finding of institutional racism in 1999). Even when crime is reported, the police may not record it: officers exercise discretion about whether an incident 'counts' as a crime; crime-recording practices have changed repeatedly (changes in 2002 and 2014 dramatically affected recorded crime figures); police priorities and targets (e.g., 'crackdowns' on drug possession) inflate statistics for those crime types. Official statistics therefore tell us as much about police behaviour and priorities as about actual crime levels.
writeBullets: Police-recorded crime — only counts crimes reported to AND recorded by the police
Dark figure of crime — all the crime that occurs but is never reported or recorded
Victims don't report: embarrassment, fear, distrust of police, don't think it's serious
Police don't record: discretion, changing recording practices, workload pressures
Police crackdowns inflate statistics for targeted crime types
Official statistics reflect police priorities as much as actual crime levels
keyPoints.memTip: Dark figure = the iceberg below the surface. Official stats only show the tip.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The dark figure is one of the highest-frequency exam concepts in this unit — ensure students can explain both why crime goes unreported AND why it goes unrecorded.

keyPoints.heading: Victim Surveys and Self-Report Studies
keyPoints.content: The Crime Survey for England and Wales (CSEW), formerly the British Crime Survey (BCS), was created precisely because official statistics were so inadequate. It annually surveys over 35,000 randomly selected households, asking respondents about their experiences of victimisation in the past year — whether or not they reported the crime to the police. Findings: total crime measured by the CSEW is consistently roughly 3–4 times higher than police-recorded crime — demonstrating the scale of the dark figure. However, the CSEW still misses: crimes without individual victims (drug use, tax evasion, insider trading), crimes respondents forget or are embarrassed to disclose (domestic violence, sexual assault), crimes against businesses (shoplifting, fraud), and murder (the victim cannot be interviewed). Self-report studies ask individuals, anonymously, about crimes they themselves have committed. Key finding: crime is much more evenly distributed across social classes and ethnic groups than official conviction statistics suggest. Middle-class crime — especially white-collar offences (fraud, tax evasion) — is massively undercounted in official statistics. This challenges the association between working-class background and criminality embedded in conviction data.
writeBullets: CSEW — surveys 35,000+ households annually about victimisation whether reported or not
CSEW finds crime is roughly 3–4× higher than police-recorded statistics
CSEW misses: victimless crimes, embarrassing crimes, crimes against businesses, murder
Self-report studies — ask individuals anonymously about crimes they have committed
Self-report finding — crime spread more evenly across classes and ethnicities than conviction data suggests
Middle-class crime (fraud, tax evasion) massively underrepresented in official statistics
keyTerms: Crime Survey for England and Wales
keyPoints.memTip: CSEW = asking people 'what happened to you?'. Self-report = asking 'what did you do?'. Both bypass police.
keyPoints.tutor_note: CSEW is a structured interview method — students can link this back to Research Methods (S3) for evaluation marks.

keyPoints.heading: Attrition and the Criminal Justice Funnel
keyPoints.content: The attrition rate refers to the way the number of criminal cases shrinks at every stage of the criminal justice process. Of all crimes committed → only some are reported → only some reported crimes are recorded → only some recorded crimes are detected (suspect identified) → only some detected crimes lead to prosecution → only some prosecutions end in conviction. The result: only a tiny fraction of all crimes committed ever results in a conviction. Differential policing: police are more likely to stop and search, arrest, and charge working-class people and ethnic minorities — meaning official statistics reflect policing choices as much as actual criminal behaviour (Home Office data 2022: Black people stopped at 7× the rate of white people). White-collar and corporate crime is massively underrepresented in official statistics: it is difficult to detect, perpetrators have power and resources to resist prosecution, and regulatory agencies (not the police) often handle it. Edwin Sutherland (1949 — who coined the term 'white-collar crime') argued that crime statistics systematically undercount crimes committed by the powerful and overcount crimes committed by the powerless. Deaths from corporate negligence (workplace accidents, pollution) significantly exceed street crime deaths but receive far less political and media attention.
writeBullets: Attrition — the funnel from crime committed → reported → recorded → detected → prosecuted → convicted
Only a tiny fraction of all crimes committed end in conviction
Differential policing — WC and ethnic minority people more likely to be stopped, arrested, charged
Official stats reflect policing choices, not just criminal behaviour
White-collar crime (Sutherland 1949) — crimes by the powerful massively underrepresented
Corporate crime deaths exceed street crime deaths but receive far less attention
keyPoints.memTip: The funnel: crimes committed → only a trickle makes it to conviction. Most crime vanishes along the way.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sutherland (1949) coined 'white-collar crime' — a key theorist to name when evaluating the class bias in crime statistics.

tables.title: Three Ways of Measuring Crime: Comparison
rows: Crimes reported to and recorded by police; published by ONS annually
Consistent over time; covers all areas nationally; free to access; allows trend analysis
Dark figure — misses unreported crime; reflects policing priorities; validity problems; recording practices change
CSEW (Crime Survey for England and Wales)
Victimisation experienced by 35,000+ households, reported or not
Captures unreported crime; large representative sample; more valid than official stats
Misses victimless crimes, crimes against businesses, murder; respondents may forget or under-disclose
Crimes individuals admit to committing, collected anonymously
Reveals hidden crime across classes and ethnicities; bypasses conviction bias
Rely on honesty; exclude very serious crimes; may under-report serious offences; small samples

1.question: The 'dark figure of crime' refers to which of the following?
options: Crime that occurs but is never reported to or recorded by the police
The total amount of crime recorded in official statistics each year
Crime committed at night, which is harder to detect
Crimes solved through forensic evidence rather than witness reports
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The dark figure is all the crime that happens but never makes it into official statistics — because victims don't report it or police don't record it. It's invisible in official data.
1.wrongFeedback: The 'dark figure' is a metaphor for crime that is hidden from view. It's not about time of day or forensics — it's about whether a crime ever gets counted at all.

2.question: Self-report studies challenge official crime statistics in a specific way. What is their key finding?
options: Crime is spread more evenly across social classes and ethnicities than conviction data suggests
Working-class people commit far more crime than middle-class people, confirming official statistics
Most crime is concentrated in city centres rather than rural areas
Women commit as much crime as men when self-report data is used
2.correctFeedback: Correct. Self-report studies find that crime — including middle-class offences like fraud and tax evasion — is much more evenly distributed than conviction statistics suggest. Official stats overcount working-class crime and undercount middle-class crime.
2.wrongFeedback: Self-report studies are specifically useful because they bypass the bias of the police and courts. They consistently find that official statistics exaggerate class and ethnic differences in offending.

commonMistakes: Saying 'the dark figure means crime is not reported' — it also includes crime that is reported but NOT recorded by the police. Both stages matter.
Thinking the CSEW is perfect — it also has blind spots (victimless crimes, murder, crimes against businesses).
Confusing crime and deviance — all crimes are deviant to some degree, but not all deviance is criminal.
Saying official statistics are wrong — they measure something (reported and recorded crime), but they measure it with significant bias. The problem is validity, not simple error.
Forgetting that changes in crime-recording practices (not just actual crime) can cause dramatic swings in official statistics.
examTips: In almost every crime and deviance question, you can mention the dark figure to show critical awareness of crime statistics — it's a reliable mark-earner.
Link the CSEW to Research Methods (S3) — it uses structured interviews with a random sample of 35,000+ households, which is good for evaluation marks.
The social construction of crime (homosexuality, cannabis, marital rape examples) is a strong opening point for any 'define crime' question.
revisionCardBullets: Deviance — breaks social norms; not necessarily illegal
Crime — breaks formal laws; carries legal punishment
Social construction of crime — what is criminal varies across time and culture (homosexuality, cannabis)
Official statistics — crimes reported to AND recorded by police; published by ONS
Dark figure of crime — crime that occurs but is never reported or recorded
Why unreported: embarrassment, fear, distrust of police, not serious enough
Why unrecorded: police discretion, changing recording practices, priorities
CSEW — 35,000+ households; asks about victimisation whether reported or not
CSEW finds: crime roughly 3–4× higher than police-recorded statistics
Self-report studies — ask individuals anonymously about crimes they committed
Self-report finding — crime more evenly spread across classes than official stats suggest
Attrition — the funnel from crime committed to conviction; only a fraction gets convicted
White-collar crime (Sutherland 1949) — crimes by the powerful massively undercounted
